discoverer: Kazimierz Fajans & Oswald Helmuth Göhring / Fredrick Soddy & John Arnold Cranston (independently)
etymology: From proto- (before) + actinium — protactinium is so named because it decays into actinium. The prefix 'proto-' indicates it is the parent (precursor) of actinium in the uranium-235 decay chain. An earlier name, 'brevium' (from Latin brevis, short) was given to the short-lived Pa-234 isotope by Fajans and Göhring in 1913.

timeline.event: Kazimierz Fajans and Oswald Helmuth Göhring, German-Polish researchers at Karlsruhe, identify a short-lived radioactive isotope (Pa-234, t½ = 1.17 minutes) in the uranium decay chain. They name it 'brevium' for its extremely short half-life. This is the first observation of element 91, but the isotope is too short-lived to characterize chemically.

Otto Hahn and Lise Meitner in Berlin, and Fredrick Soddy and John Cranston in Glasgow, simultaneously and independently identify a much longer-lived isotope — Pa-231 (t½ = 32,760 years) — in uranium ore. The long half-life allows chemical characterization. Hahn and Meitner name it 'protactinium' because it decays to actinium.

IUPAC eventually credits all four discoverers — Fajans and Göhring for the first isotope (brevium/Pa-234), and Hahn, Meitner, Soddy, and Cranston for the chemically characterized Pa-231. Hahn and Meitner's name 'protactinium' is adopted officially.

Aristid von Grosse isolates 2 milligrams of pure protactinium oxide from 0.1 grams of the element — the first isolation in visible quantity, requiring the processing of several tonnes of uranium ore residues from the Belgian Congo.

The British Atomic Energy Authority produces about 125 grams of protactinium-231 — the world's largest supply — from uranium processing residues. This sample remains the basis of virtually all Pa-231 chemistry research conducted worldwide since then.

The Pa-231/Th-230 ratio in marine sediments is developed as a paleoceanographic tracer — measuring past changes in ocean circulation. The ratio provides evidence for changes in the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation during past ice ages, becoming a key tool in paleoclimate research.

story: Protactinium's discovery is one of the most complex priority situations in element history — four different people in two different countries, working independently and simultaneously, identified different isotopes of the same element. The first observers (Fajans and Göhring) found an isotope so short-lived it couldn't be characterized chemically; the second group (Hahn, Meitner, Soddy, Cranston) found the long-lived isotope that could actually be studied. Lise Meitner, who would later co-discover nuclear fission, contributed substantially to protactinium's characterization before her most famous work. The element that precedes actinium in a radioactive decay chain now quietly enables one of the most important tools in climate science: reading past ocean circulation from sediment chemistry.
source_note: Pa-234 (brevium) identified by Fajans and Göhring (1913). Pa-231 independently identified by Hahn and Meitner (Berlin) and Soddy and Cranston (Glasgow), both 1918. Named protactinium by Hahn and Meitner.
